planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after the last frost threat; Bismarck palm grows slowly in youth but eventually becomes a dramatic, large-canopy specimen that requires ample space.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Bismarck palm is a massive, solitary-trunked fan palm from Madagascar that is grown for its extraordinarily beautiful, huge, waxy, silvery-blue to silver-gray fan leaves up to 10 feet across — the most structurally dramatic cold-tolerant palm available for zone 9–11 landscapes. Young palms are slow (1–2 feet per year) but eventually reach 30–60 feet with enormous presence. Extremely drought-tolerant once established — deeply water monthly during establishment but then largely ignore. Intolerant of sustained freezes below 22–25°F; mulch the root zone heavily in zone 9 gardens. The silvery blue-leaved cultivar 'Silver' is the most commonly grown form. A fully mature specimen is one of the most spectacular trees achievable in a subtropical landscape.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens or root suckers in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in spring; Mediterranean fan palm is one of the few multi-stemmed palms that tolerates cold winters well, surviving brief temperature dips to 5–10°F.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens or divide suckering offsets in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Mediterranean fan palm is the hardiest multi-stemmed fan palm available, native to the Mediterranean basin and tolerating brief cold snaps to 5–10°F when established — making it one of the few palms suitable for zone 8 gardens and a reliable performer through zone 11. Plants develop multiple trunks armed with sharp spines along the leaf stalks, creating a sculptural, suckering clump with dark-green to blue-green fan leaves that develops slowly to 6–15 feet. Excellent for coastal, drought-prone, or alkaline soil conditions where most palms struggle. The spiny trunk bases make it useful as a security barrier. Extremely long-lived and requires essentially no maintenance beyond removing dead fronds.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in spring; fountain grass blooms most heavily with at least 6–8 hours of direct sun daily — shade reduces flowering significantly.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; fountain grass is one of the most graceful and adaptable of all ornamental grasses, forming dense, arching mounds of narrow, green or bronze-tinted foliage with arching flower spikes capped by fluffy, bottlebrush-like, rosy-beige seed heads from midsummer through fall. The foliage turns golden-orange in fall and the dried seed heads persist through winter, providing months of four-season interest. Cut back to 4–6 inches above ground in late winter before new growth emerges. 'Hameln' (compact, 2 feet), 'Little Bunny' (miniature), and 'Moudry' (dark, nearly black seed heads) are widely grown cultivars. Note: some cultivars may self-sow and become weedy in warm climates — choose non-seeding types or remove seed heads before they mature.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Plant corms 1–2 inches deep in fall (with the flat side down) in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; winter cyclamen goes dormant in summer and should be planted where it will not receive summer irrigation.

directions: Plant corms flat-side down, 1–2 inches deep, in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; winter cyclamen (Eastern Mediterranean cyclamen) is a delightful small hardy cyclamen that blooms in late winter to early spring — often from December through February — producing tiny, swept-back, bright carmine, pink, or white flowers and beautifully marbled, kidney-shaped silver-patterned leaves that emerge in fall and persist through winter, disappearing entirely in summer dormancy. The plant naturalizes slowly by self-seeding to form expanding colonies under deciduous trees and in sheltered rock gardens. Less demanding than the tender florist's cyclamen; this species is reliably hardy and garden-worthy. Best planted in masses of 25 or more for a significant winter flowering effect.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil at the margin of a stream, pond, or bog in partial shade in spring; Japanese primrose must have constant moisture and is best established beside permanent water features.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade beside streams, ponds, or bog gardens; Japanese primrose (candelabra primrose) is the most dramatic of all primroses, producing tall stems bearing multiple stacked whorls of flowers in crimson, pink, magenta, lilac, orange, or white from late spring through early summer — each successive whorl opens above the last in the classic candelabra arrangement that gives the primrose family one of its most spectacular forms. It self-sows freely in moist, suitable conditions to colonize streambanks and pond margins. Excellent combined with ostrich ferns, hostas, and astilbes in a cool, moist, partially shaded water-garden setting. Dies back completely in hot, dry summers; consistent moisture throughout the growing season is essential. 'Miller's Crimson', 'Postford White', and 'Valley Red' are classic cultivar selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
